title: Pockets and flaps in journals
subtitle: Adding paper pockets, fold-out flaps, and tucked inserts to journal pages
excerpt: Pockets and fold-out flaps turn journal pages from flat surfaces into storage systems. A pocket inside the back cover holds loose notes; a gusseted fold-out extends a page to three times its width; a tucked insert allows a half-page to drop down. None of these require any tool beyond a bone folder, a knife, and a small amount of glue.
sourceNotes: Esther K. Smith, How to Make Books (2007, Potter Craft), chapter on inserts and additions. The gusseted fold-out technique is standard artist's book practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pleat in a fold-out insert that allows it to accordion-expand without tearing from the journal page. The gusset is a small valley fold at the join between the main page and the fold-out panel.
An element attached to a journal page by gluing only one narrow edge, leaving the rest loose so it can be lifted, flipped, or written beneath.

content.text: Each of these three additions can be made from a single sheet of 130 gsm cartridge. The only constraint is that any addition must not make the journal too thick at the foredge to close flat, keep extra pages and inserts to one or two per journal, and distribute them across the book rather than clustering them at the back.

Cut a piece of cartridge to 90% of the page width and 50% of the page height. Fold back 15 mm at the top edge (the opening) to reinforce it. Apply PVA to the bottom edge and both side edges of the piece, leaving the top fold unglued. Press this pocket onto the back cover or a rear page. The pocket opening faces upward. Let cure under a flat board for 15 minutes.
Cut a strip of paper the same height as the page and twice the page width plus 30 mm. Score and fold at the page-width point plus 15 mm (this 15 mm is the

). Fold the gusset inward as a valley fold. Apply PVA to the right-hand 15 mm edge of the piece and tip it in to the journal page: when the journal is closed, the fold-out concertinas inward; when opened, the flap extends to double the page width.

insert is any piece attached by a narrow strip of glue along one edge only. Apply PVA to a 5 mm margin at one edge of the insert and press that edge to the journal page. The insert folds flat against the page when the journal is closed. Use this for: map inserts, cut-out images, or printed grid pages added to a lined journal.

If the journal won't close flat after adding inserts, the problem is usually too many thicknesses building up near the spine. Cut each insert to stop 10 mm short of the journal spine rather than running it all the way to the binding edge. The extra gap allows the pages to close without the insert riding over the sewing.
